ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken noodle soup class (FOODON_00002700); no Thai-specific grilled chicken noodle soup subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers chicken soup broadly; no specific Thai grilled chicken noodle soup code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Thai grilled chicken noodle soup at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai grilled chicken noodle soup, approximately 450 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, and sliced grilled chicken with garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken noodle soup (Thai-style) — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken breast, rice noodles, and chicken broth
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Thai grilled chicken noodle soup entry found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data: cooked chicken breast (USDA FDC #171477), cooked rice noodles (USDA FDC #168930), and chicken broth (USDA FDC #171520), weighted to approximate a typical Thai-style grilled chicken noodle soup composition (approx. 35% broth, 35% noodles, 25% chicken, 5% garnishes). Per-100g values derived from weighted blend.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and chicken-based soups
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Thai grilled chicken noodle soup. GI estimated at 53 based on the dominant glycemic component: rice noodles (GI ~53, low GI, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). Chicken and broth contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 48.6 g) / 100 ≈ 26.0. Serving carbohydrate: 10.8 g/100g × 450 g serving = 48.6 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from broth, fish sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 450 g bowl provides approximately 1710 mg sodium, representing 74–85% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth and limit fish sauce additions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 53 (low) but a glycemic load of ~26 per bowl due to the substantial noodle portion, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced noodle portion or substitute with lower-GI konjac noodles to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat with lean grilled chicken protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled chicken breast provides lean protein with low saturated fat (~1.8 g per serving), making this dish a heart-friendly option when sodium is managed; the absence of deep-frying and minimal added oil supports a cardioprotective dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Chicken Noodle Soup (Thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวไก่ย่างน้ำซุป, romanized: Kuay Tiao Kai Yang Nam Sup) is a Thai noodle soup dish consisting of springy rice noodles served in a clear, savory chicken broth, topped with sliced grilled chicken breast. The dish is a staple of Thai street food and casual dining culture, popular in Thailand and widely available in Thai restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The broth is typically seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and white pepper, producing a light yet deeply savory flavor profile. The grilled chicken contributes a smoky, slightly charred character that distinguishes this dish from boiled-chicken noodle soups. Garnishes commonly include spring onions, bean sprouts, coriander, and dried chili flakes. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~82 kcal per 100 g), a good source of lean protein (~7.2 g per 100 g) from the grilled chicken, and moderate carbohydrate (~10.8 g per 100 g) from the rice noodles. The fat content is low (~1.4 g per 100 g), with minimal saturated fat. The primary clinical consideration is sodium: a standard bowl contains approximately 1710 mg sodium due to the seasoned broth, fish sauce, and soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low GI) based on the rice noodle component, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~26) due to the large noodle portion. This dish is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. It is not suitable for those with gluten sensitivity due to soy sauce, nor for those with fish allergies due to fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Thai grilled chicken noodle soup found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA primary databases. Nutrient values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked chicken breast, rice noodles, and chicken broth, weighted to a typical Thai-style soup composition. GI estimated from rice noodle GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. Confidence is medium due to the component-based estimation approach rather than a direct dish measurement.
notes: Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked chicken breast (FDC #171477), cooked rice noodles (FDC #168930), and chicken broth (FDC #171520), blended at approximate proportions of 25% chicken, 35% noodles, 35% broth, and 5% garnishes by weight. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimation and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the blended component sources at sufficient confidence for inclusion. Trans fat is 0.0 as grilled chicken breast and rice noodles contain negligible trans fat; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA SR Legacy source data. GI source is analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI (53, low) from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Sodium content is notably elevated due to seasoned broth and condiments; clinical guidance for hypertension and cardiovascular disease reflects this. The food_name has been normalized from the menu-style label 'N5 Soup Grilled Chicken Noodle' to the canonical English form 'Grilled Chicken Noodle Soup'; the original menu name is preserved in aliases.
